name: Discretionary Propensity to Consume, Case II

book_quotes.quote: c ≡ (x1 – x1min)/(x1max – x1min) = (p1x1 – p1x1min)/(y – p1x1min), so that 0 ≤ c ≤ 1.

The same result obtains if instead c declines with discretionary income. To see this, we rewrite equation (3.4) as (p1x1 – p1x1min) = c (y – p1x1min), which is a linear relationship between discretionary expenditure on necessary goods and discretionary income. Since c is the slope of this curve, as c falls the curve gets flatter.

Figures 3.3–3.5 display the results of the case in which x1min rises more slowly than income, figures 3.6–3.7 the case in which c declines with income, and figures 3.8 and 3.9 the characteristic patterns in actual data.

primary_source.publication: Shaikh, Anwar. 2016. Capitalism: Competition, Conflict, Crises. New York: Oxford University Press.
primary_source.units: discretionary propensity c (dimensionless 0–0.8) plotted against income y (0–60 model units)
primary_source.license: Book figure; reproduction governed by OUP permissions

formula: c(y) declining function of y, illustrating Case II (alternative path to Engel-curve saturation in which c itself falls with income while x1min is held constant).
methodology_notes: Fig 3.6 illustrates Case II: c declines with income while x1min is constant. It is the partner to Fig 3.7 (the resulting Engel curve).
Stub set content_type='time_series'; re-typed to 'theoretical'. The abscissa is a synthetic income grid, not historical time.
